title: Symbols 🔮
description: Meet the unique, memory-saving data type perfect for Hashes.

slides.title: Strings are Heavy 🧱
slides.content:
Using a String like `"name"` as a Hash Key works totally fine.

But Strings are heavy! Every single time you type `"name"`, Ruby creates a brand new, unique string in your computer's memory.

If you have 10,000 hashes that all use the key `"name"`, you are wasting a massive amount of memory doing nothing.

slides.title: Enter: Symbols 🔮
slides.content:
A **Symbol** looks like a string, but it is preceded by a colon `:` and has no quotes.

`:name` 

A symbol is a lightweight, immutable (unchangeable) string. Once you create `:name`, Ruby creates it in memory exactly ONE time, and reuses it endlessly!

This makes them the absolute perfect tool to use as Hash Keys.

slides.title: Hashes with Symbols
slides.content: Notice how we use symbols as the keys instead of strings, making our program vastly faster and more memory-efficient.

quizData.question: What is the primary benefit of using a Symbol over a String for a Hash Key in Ruby?

options.text: Symbols are prettier to look at.
Symbols save massive amounts of memory because they are only created once and reused seamlessly.
Strings cannot be used as Hash Keys at all.

quizData.explanation: Symbols are immutable and reused endlessly. You use them when the text's only purpose is to act as a permanent label/identifier.

slides.title: The Modern JSON Syntax 🚀
slides.content:
Because 99% of Ruby developers use Symbols for their Hash Keys, Ruby created a brand new, ultra-fast shorthand syntax!

You drop the hash rocket `=>` entirely, and put the colon *at the end* of the symbol name. 

```ruby
student = { name: "Alice", age: 22 }
```

This looks drastically cleaner, and mimics the syntax used in JavaScript and JSON.

slides.title: Shorthand Magic
slides.content:
This is how you will see Hashes written in all professional Ruby code.
Notice that when you RETRIEVE the data, the colon goes back to the front `[:name]`.

quizData.question: In the modern syntax `user = { name: "John" }`, what data type is the key?
quizData.explanation: Even though the colon moved to the back, Ruby automatically converts `name:` into the Symbol `:name` beneath the surface.
